mnemonics.title: API Capacity Factors
mnemonics.content: Remember the 5 C's of API Capacity: Cost (resources), Concurrency (connections), Caching (efficiency), Complexity (parsing overhead), and Connections (maintenance).

mnemonics.title: Rate Limiting Types
mnemonics.content: LEARN Rate Limiting: Leaky Bucket (smooth output), Enhanced Token Bucket (burst allowed), Adaptive (dynamic), Rate (fixed window), and Nested (hierarchical).

mnemonics.title: API Monitoring Metrics
mnemonics.content: Remember RED Method: Rate (requests/sec), Errors (error rate), Duration (latency). Also remember USE: Utilization, Saturation, Errors.

mnemonics.title: Caching Levels
mnemonics.content: Think CACHE: Client-side (local), CDN (edge), Application (in-memory), Database (query), and HTTP (response).

acronyms.usage: The contract between two software systems enabling communication
Key throughput metric for measuring API capacity
Duration for which cached data remains valid before refresh
Compact, URL-safe token format for secure API authentication
Distributed network of servers for caching API responses geographically
Two-way authentication for high-security API communication
Contract defining acceptable API performance levels
Techniques to prioritize API traffic and guarantee performance

visualTips.visualize: Imagine a water tank with a small hole - requests enter as water, leaky bucket allows steady drip (processed), overflow goes to overflow tank (throttled/rejected).
Draw an inverted pyramid: Client cache at top (smallest, fastest), CDN below, Application cache in middle, Database at bottom (largest, slowest). Each layer catches requests before they go deeper.
Think of an electrical circuit breaker - CLOSED (normal flow), OPEN (stopped due to failures), HALF-OPEN (testing if recovery happened).
Think of a toll booth with multiple lanes - requests are distributed like cars across available servers to prevent any single lane from jamming.
Imagine a bucket with tokens: each request needs a token, tokens are added at fixed rate, bucket has max capacity, excess tokens overflow (lost). Burst allowed up to bucket size.

analogies.analogy: Like a theme park entrance - only a certain number of guests (requests) allowed per hour, excess must wait in queue or come back later.
Like a restaurant's prep station - frequently requested items (popular dishes) are prepared in advance (cached), reducing cooking time (server processing) for each order.
Like a hotel concierge - single point of contact handling check-in (authentication), directing to rooms (routing), and managing guest limits (rate limiting).
REST is like ordering a pre-made combo meal - you get everything (over-fetching). GraphQL is like ordering from a buffet - pick exactly what you want (precise fetching).
Like a fuse in electrical system - when circuit fails repeatedly (short circuit), fuse breaks (opens) to prevent fire (system-wide failure), then can be reset.
Like an ID badge with embedded information - security guard (server) validates it locally without checking central database every time, saving time.
